annotations.label: Graph

anchor.snippet: A graph (sometimes called an undirected graph to distinguish it from a directed graph

annotations.note: Mathlib's `SimpleGraph V` is a structure with a symmetric irreflexive adjacency relation, matching the undirected simple-graph definition.

annotations.label: Empty graph

anchor.snippet: An empty graph is a graph that has an empty set of vertices

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.emptyGraph V := ⊥` is the graph with no edges (note Mathlib's empty graph has empty edge set rather than empty vertex set).

annotations.label: Order of a graph

anchor.snippet: The order of a graph is its number

annotations.note: Mathlib has no dedicated `order` of a graph but expresses it as `Fintype.card V` on the vertex type.

annotations.label: Size of a graph

anchor.snippet: The size of a graph is its number

annotations.note: There is no `size` decl but the cardinality `#G.edgeFinset` plays this role via `SimpleGraph.edgeFinset_card`.

annotations.label: Degree of a vertex

anchor.snippet: The degree or valency of a vertex is the number of edges that are incident to it

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.degree v := #(G.neighborFinset v)` counts edges incident to `v`.

annotations.label: Endpoints of an edge

anchor.snippet: are called the edge's endpoints

annotations.note: Edges live in `Sym2 V`, so endpoints are recovered by membership in the unordered pair rather than via a dedicated `endpoints` definition.

annotations.label: Isolated vertex

anchor.snippet: it is not joined to any other vertex and is called isolated

annotations.note: No `IsolatedVertex` predicate found; one would have to spell out `G.degree v = 0` or `G.neighborSet v = ∅`.

annotations.label: Adjacent vertices

anchor.snippet: the vertices u and v are called adjacent

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.Adj : V → V → Prop` is the adjacency relation defining when two vertices are joined by an edge.

annotations.label: Multigraph

anchor.snippet: A multigraph is a generalization that allows multiple edges to have the same pair of endpoints

annotations.note: `Graph α β` formalizes multigraphs with vertex set in `α` and edge set in `β`, allowing parallel edges and loops.

annotations.label: Graph with loops

anchor.snippet: Sometimes, graphs are allowed to contain loops

annotations.note: `Graph` permits loops via `Graph.IsLoopAt e x := G.IsLink e x x`; `Digraph` also permits self-loops.

annotations.label: Maximum degree and edges in graph of order n

anchor.snippet: In a graph of order n , the maximum degree of each vertex is

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.maxDegree_lt_card_verts` states the maximum degree is less than the number of vertices.

annotations.label: Adjacency relation and matrix

anchor.snippet: The edges of a graph define a symmetric relation on the vertices, called the adjacency relation

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.Adj` is the symmetric adjacency relation and `SimpleGraph.adjMatrix` is the corresponding symmetric 0/1 matrix.

annotations.label: Directed graph (digraph)

anchor.snippet: A directed graph or digraph is a graph in which edges have orientations

annotations.note: `Digraph V` wraps an arbitrary binary relation `Adj : V → V → Prop`, modelling directed graphs (with self-loops allowed).

annotations.label: Directed simple graph

anchor.snippet: a directed graph is a pair

annotations.note: `Digraph V` (a relation on `V`) gives exactly the pair (vertex set, ordered-pair edge set) of a directed simple graph.

annotations.label: Endpoints, tail, and head of a directed edge

anchor.snippet: the vertices x and y are called the endpoints of the edge

annotations.note: In `Quiver V`, an edge `f : X ⟶ Y` has tail `X` and head `Y` recoverable as the type indices, but there is no separately named `tail`/`head` accessor.

annotations.label: Inverted edge

anchor.snippet: is called the inverted edge of

annotations.note: `Quiver.reverse` (under `HasReverse`) gives the inverted arrow `b ⟶ a` of `a ⟶ b`.

annotations.label: Multiple edges in a directed graph

anchor.snippet: Multiple edges , not allowed under the definition above, are two or more edges with both the same tail and the same head

annotations.note: `Quiver` permits multiple edges (`X ⟶ Y` is a `Sort`, not a `Prop`), but there is no named `MultipleEdge` predicate.

annotations.label: Directed multigraph

anchor.snippet: a directed graph is sometimes defined to be an ordered triple

annotations.note: `Quiver V` provides, for each pair `(a,b)`, a type of arrows `a ⟶ b`, exactly modelling a directed multigraph.

annotations.label: Loop in directed graph

anchor.snippet: A loop is an edge that joins a vertex to itself

annotations.note: `Graph.IsLoopAt e x := G.IsLink e x x` captures an edge joining a vertex to itself; `Digraph` also permits self-loops via its general adjacency relation.

annotations.label: Adjacency relation of directed graph

anchor.snippet: The edges of a directed simple graph permitting loops

annotations.note: `Digraph` IS a binary relation `Adj : V → V → Prop` on the vertex set, which is the adjacency relation of a directed simple graph permitting loops.

annotations.label: Mixed graph

anchor.snippet: A mixed graph is a graph in which some edges may be directed and some may be undirected

annotations.note: No `MixedGraph` structure found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Weighted graph

anchor.snippet: A weighted graph or a network

annotations.note: No dedicated `WeightedGraph` type; edge-weighting would be encoded as a function on `edgeSet` ad hoc.

annotations.label: Oriented graph

anchor.snippet: One definition of an oriented graph is that it is a directed graph in which at most one of

annotations.note: `Mathlib.Combinatorics.Digraph.Orientation` discusses orientation-forgetting maps, but does not define a standalone `OrientedGraph` predicate.

annotations.label: Regular graph

anchor.snippet: A regular graph is a graph in which each vertex has the same number of neighbours

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.IsRegularOfDegree d` asserts every vertex has degree `d`.

annotations.label: Complete graph

anchor.snippet: A complete graph is a graph in which each pair of vertices is joined by an edge

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.completeGraph V := ⊤` is the simple graph in which every distinct pair is adjacent.

annotations.label: Finite graph

anchor.snippet: A finite graph is a graph in which the vertex set and the edge set are finite sets

annotations.note: Finiteness is expressed via typeclasses `[Fintype V]` / `[Fintype G.edgeSet]` rather than a `FiniteGraph` definition.

annotations.label: Connected unordered pair of vertices

anchor.snippet: an unordered pair of vertices

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.Reachable u v` expresses the existence of a walk between `u` and `v`, i.e. that the pair is connected.

annotations.label: Connected graph

anchor.snippet: A connected graph is an undirected graph in which every unordered pair of vertices in the graph is connected

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.Connected` is `Preconnected ∧ Nonempty V`, requiring every two vertices to be reachable.

annotations.label: Strongly and weakly connected ordered pair

anchor.snippet: an ordered pair of vertices

annotations.note: No `Digraph.Reachable`/`StronglyConnected`/`WeaklyConnected` predicate for ordered pairs in directed graphs found.

annotations.label: Strongly connected graph

anchor.snippet: A strongly connected graph is a directed graph in which every ordered pair of vertices in the graph is strongly connected

annotations.note: No `StronglyConnected` predicate on `Digraph` found in Mathlib (the term appears only for matrices/categories).

annotations.label: k-vertex-connected graph

anchor.snippet: A k-vertex-connected graph or k-edge-connected graph is a graph in which no set of

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `SimpleGraph.IsEdgeConnected k` (k-edge-connectivity) but no analogous `IsVertexConnected` predicate.

annotations.label: Bipartite graph

anchor.snippet: A bipartite graph is a simple graph in which the vertex set can be partitioned into two sets

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.IsBipartite` (an abbreviation for `Colorable 2`) and `IsBipartiteWith s t` capture bipartite graphs.

annotations.label: Complete bipartite graph

anchor.snippet: In a complete bipartite graph , the vertex set is the union of two disjoint sets

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.completeBipartiteGraph V W` on `V ⊕ W` adjoins every cross-side pair.

annotations.label: Path graph

anchor.snippet: A path graph or linear graph of order

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.pathGraph n` is the path graph on `Fin n` with adjacency `u+1=v ∨ v+1=u`.

annotations.label: Characterization of path graphs

anchor.snippet: Path graphs can be characterized as connected graphs in which the degree of all but two vertices is 2

annotations.note: No `iff`-style characterization of `pathGraph` by its degree sequence is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Planar graph

anchor.snippet: A planar graph is a graph whose vertices and edges can be drawn in a plane such that no two of the edges intersect

annotations.note: No `Planar` predicate or planar-graph theory exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cycle graph

anchor.snippet: A cycle graph or circular graph of order

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.cycleGraph n` is defined on `Fin n` as the circulant graph with connection set `{1}`.

annotations.label: Characterization of cycle graphs

anchor.snippet: Cycle graphs can be characterized as connected graphs in which the degree of all vertices is 2

annotations.note: No theorem in Mathlib characterizing cycle graphs as connected 2-regular graphs was found.

annotations.label: Tree

anchor.snippet: A tree is an undirected graph in which any two vertices are connected by exactly one path

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.IsTree` requires the graph to be connected and acyclic, with `IsTree.existsUnique_path` giving the unique-path characterization.

annotations.label: Forest

anchor.snippet: A forest is an undirected graph in which any two vertices are connected by at most one path

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.IsAcyclic` is Mathlib's name for a forest (no cyclic walks); `isAcyclic_iff_path_unique` proves the at-most-one-path characterization.

annotations.label: Polytree

anchor.snippet: A polytree (or directed tree or oriented tree or singly connected network ) is a directed acyclic graph

annotations.note: No `Polytree` (DAG whose underlying graph is a tree) decl found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polyforest

anchor.snippet: A polyforest (or directed forest or oriented forest ) is a directed acyclic graph whose underlying undirected graph is a forest

annotations.note: No `Polyforest` decl was found in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adjacent and consecutive vertices

anchor.snippet: Two vertices of a graph are called adjacent if they share a common edge

annotations.note: Adjacency of vertices is `SimpleGraph.Adj`; 'consecutive' along a walk is captured implicitly by `Walk.cons`.

annotations.label: Trivial, edgeless, and null graph

anchor.snippet: The graph with only one vertex and no edges is called the trivial graph

annotations.note: `emptyGraph` covers the edgeless case for any vertex type, but Mathlib has no dedicated name for the trivial (single-vertex) graph.

annotations.label: Labeled and unlabeled graphs

anchor.snippet: This kind of graph may be called vertex-labeled

annotations.note: Labels/isomorphism classes are not given a dedicated definition; labeling is implicit in the vertex type.

annotations.label: Category of directed multigraphs as comma category

anchor.snippet: The category of directed multigraphs permitting loops is the comma category

annotations.note: `Quiv` is defined as a category and has a forget/free adjunction with `Cat`, but Mathlib does not identify it with the comma category `Set ↓ D × D`.

annotations.label: Binary relation defines a directed graph

anchor.snippet: A binary relation R on a set X defines a directed graph

annotations.note: `Digraph V` is literally a wrapper around a binary relation `Adj : V → V → Prop`, embodying this correspondence.

annotations.label: Directed graphs model information networks

anchor.snippet: A directed graph can model information networks such as Twitter

annotations.note: This is an informal application example with no corresponding Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Cayley and Schreier coset graphs

anchor.snippet: Particularly regular examples of directed graphs are given by the Cayley graphs of finitely-generated groups

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.mulCayley` formalizes Cayley graphs and `Quiver.SchreierGraph` (Mathlib.Combinatorics.Quiver.Schreier) formalizes Schreier coset graphs.

annotations.label: Small category as directed multigraph

anchor.snippet: every small category has an underlying directed multigraph whose vertices are the objects of the category

annotations.note: `CategoryTheory.Quiv.forget : Cat ⥤ Quiv` is the forgetful functor sending each small category to its underlying quiver (directed multigraph).

annotations.label: Hypergraph

anchor.snippet: In a hypergraph , an edge can join any positive number of vertices

annotations.note: No `Hypergraph` type appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph as simplicial complex

anchor.snippet: An undirected graph can be seen as a simplicial complex consisting of

annotations.note: Mathlib has `SimplicialComplex` but no canonical map turning a `SimpleGraph` into one.

annotations.label: Every graph gives rise to a matroid

anchor.snippet: Every graph gives rise to a matroid

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Matroid` but no `graphicMatroid`/`cycleMatroid` construction from a graph was found.
